name: Drakmari
description:
The Drakmari evolved in the abyssal trenches of Pelagis, a high-gravity ocean world with no continents and no sunlight below the first kilometre of water. In those crushing depths, where pressure would flatten steel and darkness is absolute, the Drakmari became apex predators of terrifying efficiency — bioluminescent, pressure-resistant, with layered cartilaginous armour and a lateral sensory array that can detect a heartbeat through a kilometre of water. They are not large by galactic standards, but they are built for ambush, and the evolutionary arms race of Pelagis's deep trenches produced a species with reflexes, tactical instincts, and spatial awareness that translate disturbingly well to naval combat.

The Drakmari came to space because their ocean is dying. Two centuries of industrialisation — the Drakmari industrialised late but catastrophically fast — has poisoned the deep trenches that were their ancestral home. Thermal vents are clogged with heavy metals. Microbial mats that formed the base of the food chain are collapsing. The population of Pelagis has quadrupled in three generations, and the ocean that sustained them for millions of years cannot sustain them for another century. The Drakmari do not speak of this openly. In their culture, to admit weakness is to invite predation. But every Drakmari who boards a starship knows the truth: they are not exploring. They are fleeing. They need new oceans, new worlds with deep water and high pressure, or their species will starve in the dark.

Drakmari culture is built around the Hunt — not as mere predation but as a philosophy of existence. The Hunt is how the Drakmari understand honour, duty, beauty, and meaning. A well-executed ambush is art. A prey animal that fights back brilliantly earns more respect than one that submits. The Drakmari do not despise their enemies; they study them, admire their strengths, and then kill them with precision and something approaching reverence. Their code of conduct, the Deep Law, governs everything from combat to courtship. You do not strike a foe who cannot fight back. You do not poison a hunting ground. You do not take more than you need. The bitter irony — that their industrial pollution violates every principle of the Deep Law — is the great unspoken shame of Drakmari civilisation.

This shame has fractured them. The Abyssal Traditionalists demand a return to the old ways — sustainable harvesting, population control, the abandonment of heavy industry. They would rather the species shrank than betrayed the Deep Law further. The Current-Riders, the progressive faction, argue that survival requires adaptation and that the Deep Law must evolve as the Drakmari evolve — that finding new oceans among the stars is not a betrayal of tradition but its ultimate expression. Beneath both factions lies a darker current: the Depth-Callers, a secretive movement that believes the Drakmari were never meant to leave their trenches, that the stars are a prey-ground too vast and too alien, and that the species should return to the deep and accept whatever comes. No one admits to being a Depth-Caller. But their symbols — old glyphs from the lightless trenches — keep appearing on bulkheads, scratched in the dark.
